 2. Use `hexi::span` to get a span of that size from the beginning of the buffer.
 3. Construct a PacketHeader from the span's data.

 But note: the `span` function in Hexi advances the internal state of the buffer? Actually, looking at the provided code for `span` and `view`, they do not modify the buffer they are called on. They just return a view.

   template<typename out_type = value_type>
   std::span<out_type> span(size_type count) requires contiguous<buf_type>

 It returns a span of `out_type` (defaults to `value_type` which is `uint8_t` in our case) of `count` elements.

#include <vector>

std::vector<uint8_t> read_buffer_data(const std::vector<uint8_t>& source_buffer, size_t bytes_to_read) {

    hexi::buffer_adaptor<std::vector<uint8_t>> adaptor(source_buffer);
    
    // Create a destination vector of the required size

    return dest;
}

    // Write the source_data to the destination buffer
    adaptor.write(source_data.data(), source_data.size());
}
